summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's record Q1 2008 financial results, focusing on three key areas: iPod economics, iPhone momentum, and iTunes Store growth. He argues that while iPod unit sales growth has slowed dramatically (from 50% to 5%), revenue growth remains steady at 17% because Apple is selling more expensive devices like the iPod Touch. He makes the prescient observation that the iPhone and iPod Touch constitute a single unnamed mobile software platform that is already outselling Macs, and predicts a coming gold rush for third-party app developers. He also highlights the iTunes Store's 27% revenue growth as an underappreciated sign of strength.
key_points: iPod unit sales growth slowed from 50% to 5% year-over-year, but revenue growth held steady at 17%, driven by higher-priced devices like the iPod Touch
The iPod should not be thought of as a digital Walkman destined for commodity pricing, but as 'the best pocket-sized anti-boredom device Apple can make'
iPhone and iPod Touch constitute a single unnamed mobile OS X platform that already outsells Macs in unit volume
Apple's cut of third-party app sales through the iTunes Store represents a potential gold rush for indie Mac developers
iTunes Store revenue growth of 27% is at least as impressive as the Mac's 47% revenue growth given the store's maturity
money_quotes: How do you grow revenue faster than unit sales? By selling more iPods at the high-end of the product line. Hello, iPod Touch.
It's wrong to think of the iPod simply as a digital Walkman. Better to think of the iPod as, say, 'the best pocket-sized anti-boredom device Apple can make'.
In 2001, that device was a simple hard-drive based 5 GB MP3 player. Today, it's the iPhone and iPod Touch.
Apple sold almost exactly the same number of iPhones as Macs: 2,315,000 vs. 2,319,000. That's impressive in and of itself.
I don't see how anyone could doubt that the iPhone is a smash hit at this point.
Even in a quarter with 44 percent higher year-over-year Mac unit sales, Apple sold more 'Mobile OS X' devices than Macs.
This is a potential gold rush for indie Mac developers.
27 percent growth sounds pretty damn solid for the four-year-old market-leading music store.
key_insight: The iPod's slowing unit growth masked a strategic shift toward higher-value devices like the iPod Touch and iPhone, which together formed a new mobile platform already outselling Macs and poised to create an entirely new software economy.
